1:
The chapter opens with the aged king turning once more to the assembled nation, and the witnesses gather first around the man Solomon and the work laid on him. The reformed John Gill notes only that David, having finished his charge to Solomon, now addresses the whole congregation again, the transition from private counsel to public appeal. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the sense of the king's words: Solomon is "young and tender," and "alone," that is, chosen out of all his brothers, for the one great task; and the work is great, "for the palace is not for man, but for the LORD God." Ellicott lingers over that word "palace" (Hebrew birah), a term peculiar to Chronicles, Nehemiah, Esther and Daniel, which usually names the fortified palace at Susa; this is the only place in Scripture where it denotes the Temple, and its august associations, he suggests, were well fitted to convey to the chronicler's own contemporaries some idea of the magnificence of Solomon's house as he imagined it, a point he grounds in 1 Chron. 22:5.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the verse out into a lesson on provoking one another to love and good works. It is our duty, he says, not only to do good ourselves but to draw in others to do good too; and though David would not impose a tax upon the rich men of Israel, he recommends the building of the Temple as a fair occasion for a free-will offering, because what is done in works of piety and charity should be done willingly and not by constraint, for God loves a cheerful giver. Henry marks the reasons David presses: Solomon is young and tender and needs help, yet is the man God has chosen; the palace is not for man but for the Lord God, so the more is contributed the more magnificent it will be; and it is good service to encourage those who are as yet young and tender in the work of God.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the verse in its place, noting that here David enumerates the gifts he designed for the building and exhorts princes and people to make their own offerings. He too seizes on the Targum's reading of the last clause, "the palace is not prepared for the name of a son of man, but for the name of the Word of the Lord God," a paraphrase that lifts the whole enterprise above every merely human dignity. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the note the chapter will sustain, that the house is God's and not man's, and that the giving toward it must flow from a willing and affectionate heart.
2:
David turns to the inventory of what he has laid up, and the witnesses read the long catalogue of materials as the visible shape of a whole-hearted devotion. The reformed John Gill glosses the opening line simply, "I have prepared with all my might for the house of my God," that is, to the very utmost of his ability, for the building and the decorating of it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the Hebrew, "and with all might have I prepared," and unfolds the list that follows: "the gold for the gold and the silver for the silver," the metal set apart for every golden and silver thing, and then the precious stones whose very names, he confesses, are uncertain.

Ellicott dwells on that uncertainty with a philologist's candor. The "onyx" (Hebrew shoham) is rendered by the Septuagint in different places as onyx, beryl, sardius, emerald and sapphire, so that its precise stone can hardly be fixed. "Stones to be set" are literally stones of "fillings," gems for their settings. The "glistering stones, and of divers colours" are stones of puk and riqmah: puk, he explains, is the very pigment Eastern ladies used to darken the eyebrows and lashes, the kohl of 2 Kings 9:30, here naming the colour of the stones, perhaps a decorative marble; and riqmah stones are veined or variegated marbles, or tessellated work. The last item, "marble stones," renders shayish, a word read only here, meaning white marble.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Keil and Delitzsch, keeps the whole list in view, gold, silver, brass, iron, and wood, each for its own kind of work, with onyx and setting-stones and glistering stones of divers colours and marble in abundance, and reads it as the sum of David's provision for the several buildings of the temple. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the marble at the end. The Hebrew shayish, he argues, is the marmor Parium of the Vulgate, Parian marble from the island of Paros among the Cyclades, which produced the whitest and finest marble of antiquity, the stone of which most of the noblest ancient works were made; and he recalls Josephus, who reports that the temple was built of great blocks of white polished marble that made a most splendid appearance.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn a dry list of materials into a portrait of magnificence prepared beforehand, gold and silver by the ton, iron and brass without number, and marble white as the finest of the ancient world, all gathered, as Gill says, with all the king's might, because the house was for the LORD and nothing less than the best would serve it.
3:
The verse turns from the public store to the king's own purse, and the two witnesses at hand read it as the proof of David's affection put into gold and silver. The reformed John Gill fixes on the opening motive, "because I have set my affection to the house of my God," that David had a hearty good will toward it, and was earnestly desirous of seeing it built, and built in a grand and magnificent manner. The affection is not a passing sentiment but a settled love that now translates itself into a costly gift.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the Hebrew of that love and that giving. "I have set my affection" renders a verb of liking or delighting (ratsah), the same warmth of the psalmist who loved the habitation of God's house; and the gift itself David describes as "mine own proper good, of gold and silver," a personal property, the word segullah, the peculiar treasure or peculium that is a man's very own. What David gives is not drawn from the public levy already gathered for the work, but from his private fortune, offered "over and above" (lemalah) all that he had prepared for the holy house. Ellicott notes too the fine present force of the verb: not merely "I have given," but "I give," the offering made in the very act of speaking, laid down before the assembly then and there.

Brief as the verse is, the witnesses let its weight show. David has already prepared, with all his might, the vast common store of the previous verses; now he adds to it, from his own reserved treasure, a further and freer gift, and he does so for one reason only, that he has set his heart upon the house of his God. The private gift, offered on top of the public provision, is the measure of the affection; a man's own peculiar treasure, the thing most nearly his, is precisely what he lays on the altar of a love that will not be satisfied with what duty alone requires. So the verse prepares the ground for the great sums about to be named, and for the appeal that will follow, "who then is willing?", by showing the king himself first willing, and willing at his own personal cost.
4:
Now the king names the figures, and the witnesses divide their labour between the staggering sums and the meaning of the gift. The reformed John Gill and the grammatical-historical witnesses all reach for the ready-reckoner. Gill reports that the three thousand talents of gold amounted, by Scheuchzer's calculation, to some thirty-six million ducats, or by Brerewood's to thirteen and a half million pounds. Keil and Delitzsch reckon the same three thousand talents at roughly thirteen and a half million pounds, or, by the royal shekel, six and three-quarter millions, and the seven thousand talents of silver at some two and a half millions; Charles Ellicott sets the gold at about eighteen million sterling and the silver at nearly three millions. The exact figure escapes them, but the order of magnitude does not: this is a king's private benefaction of almost unimaginable weight.

On the gold itself the witnesses agree that "the gold of Ophir" means the finest and purest. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says flatly it is "the best and purest gold," and that the walls of the temple and of the rooms adjoining were to be overlaid with silver beaten out into plates. Ellicott locates Ophir more precisely as the source of Indian gold, from Abhira at the mouth of the Indus. Keil and Delitzsch explain the purpose: the verb, "to overlay," means to cover the inner walls of the temple buildings with leaf of gold and silver; the walls of the holy place and the most holy, of the porch and of the upper chambers, were overlaid with gold, and probably the inner walls of the side-buildings as well. Ellicott renders the same verb bluntly as "to besmear," and takes "the houses" to be the chambers of the temple, a sense he ties to 1 Chron. 22:14.

One textual curiosity the witnesses record without resolving: Ellicott notes that the Syriac and Arabic versions read not three thousand but "a thousand thousand talents of gold," and "twice a thousand thousand talents of silver," a wild inflation of an already vast sum, which he lets stand as a variant rather than a correction. The value of the bronze and iron, he adds elsewhere, must have been far greater then than now.

Beneath the arithmetic the witnesses share a single point. These are not gifts wrung from the treasury by tax, but gold and silver David has reserved from his own store and now pours out upon the walls of a house he will never see finished. The purest gold of the earth, beaten thin and laid over every wall of the holy place, is the outward sign of an inward affection, the king spending his best, and his own, upon the habitation of his God.
5:
The catalogue closes and the appeal opens, and the witnesses gather around the arresting Hebrew idiom behind the call to give. The reformed John Gill glosses the first clause plainly, the gold is for the things to be overlaid with gold, the silver for the things of silver, and then the king turns to the assembly: "And who then is willing to consecrate his service this day unto the LORD?" The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical witnesses all pause over the phrase "consecrate his service," which is literally "to fill his hand." Clarke renders it lemalloth yado, "to fill his hand," that is, to bring an offering to the Lord. Ellicott reads it "who volunteers to fill his hand today for Jehovah," to fill it, that is, with a liberal offering, a gesture he traces to Exodus 32:29. Keil and Delitzsch give the same sense: to fill one's hand to the Lord means to provide oneself with something which one then brings to him.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the practical lesson out of the idiom. "To consecrate," he says, is "to offer an offering, as I have done"; and the Hebrew, "to fill his hand unto the Lord," carries a truth about all service: they that engage themselves in the service of God "will have their hands full," for there is work enough for the whole man in that service. The metaphor of the filled hand becomes an image of a life wholly occupied with God.

Keil and Delitzsch mark the grammar of the appeal, the little connective that presents the question as the natural consequence of all that has gone before: David has given, the materials are ready, and so, who will now come forward? The verb "to fill," they note, is a rare form, found also in the account of the tabernacle's craftsmen and in Daniel.

The witnesses do not divide, and the force of the verse lies where they all place it. David does not command a levy; he issues an invitation, and he issues it having first filled his own hands. The one who asks "who is willing?" has already answered the question with his own vast gift, so that the appeal comes not from a throne demanding tribute but from a giver inviting others to share his joy. To fill the hand for the LORD is to bring something to him freely; and the king, having filled his own, now waits to see whose hands will be filled beside his.
6:
The appeal is answered, and the witnesses watch the leaders of Israel come forward in a body. The reformed John Gill identifies the ranks briefly, the chief of the fathers and the princes of the twelve tribes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the several titles the English piles up, chief, princes, captains, rulers, all render a single Hebrew word, sarim, the great men of the realm; and that the verb translated "offered willingly" means they "showed themselves liberal," nadib, the same generous spirit David had appealed to. The "rulers of the king's work," he explains, are the stewards or bailiffs of the royal domains, the officials over David's fields and herds and vineyards catalogued earlier in the book.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Keil and Delitzsch, records the sums, five thousand talents of gold and ten thousand darics, and enters into a careful note on that coin. The Hebrew adarkon, they insist, does not correspond to the Greek drachma or the Arabic dirhem, but to the daric, the dareikos, as the Syriac rendering in Ezra 8:27 confirms: a Persian gold coin worth something over twenty shillings, of which several specimens still survive. The princes, in other words, followed the king's example not grudgingly but with a liberality measured in tons of gold.

The witnesses do not divide over the fact, though a difficulty lurks in that word "daric" which the next verse will make sharper, for the Persian coin belongs to an age long after David. Here the point is simply the response: David had first filled his own hands, and now the heads of the tribes, the commanders of the army, and the overseers of the royal estates fill theirs beside him. The appeal for a free-will offering does not fall on deaf ears; the leadership of the nation rises as one to match their king, so that the burden of the great house is shouldered willingly by all who are able. What David modelled in the previous verses, the princes here embody, a giving that is neither taxed nor constrained, but offered, as Ellicott says, out of a liberal and willing heart.
7:
The totals mount, and the witnesses busy themselves both with the reckoning of the sums and with a single anachronistic word buried in them. The reformed John Gill notes only the purpose of the giving, for the building and adorning of the house and the providing of its utensils. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke turns his page into a ledger: the five thousand talents of gold he computes at more than twenty-five million pounds sterling, the ten thousand drams as golden darics worth about a pound each, the ten thousand talents of silver at some three and a half millions, the eighteen thousand talents of brass at over a thousand tons, and the hundred thousand talents of iron at more than five thousand seven hundred tons. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the gold, more loosely, at between thirty and forty millions sterling, and marks the sum with a startled exclamation.

It is Ellicott who fastens on the word "drams," which he insists should read "darics." The daric, he explains, was a Persian gold coin worth about a pound and two shillings, first struck by Darius the son of Hystaspes, and current in Western Asia long after the fall of the Persian empire; the Hebrew adarkonim occurs elsewhere only at Ezra 8:27, where it plainly means darics. And here Ellicott draws his critical conclusion: no real coins are mentioned anywhere in Scripture before the age of the exile, so that the chronicler, or his authority, has evidently put a familiar later term in place of some older and now unknown expression of value. Keil and Delitzsch, at the previous verse, had made the same identification of the coin, though without pressing the point of its lateness.

Beneath the numbers and the coin the witnesses see the same glad scene. Keil and Delitzsch note that those with whom precious stones were found gave them to the treasure of the house of the LORD, into the charge of Jehiel the Gershonite; and that the people rejoiced, for they had offered willingly, with a perfect heart, so that David the king also rejoiced with great joy. The vast catalogue of metals is not, in the end, an accountant's boast but a record of overflowing gladness, a whole nation emptying its treasuries with a single mind. Whether the "daric" is David's word or the chronicler's, the substance the witnesses affirm is the willing heart behind the gift, the joy of a people who found they had both the means and the mind to give.
8:
A single further class of givers is singled out, and the two witnesses at hand attend to who they were and where their gift was stored. The reformed John Gill passes over the verse quickly, noting only that these were such as had precious stones like those named before. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the Hebrew more closely: "and with whom there was found stones, they gave unto the treasure," the construction using the article as a relative, as it does again in the verses on either side. The stones went, he explains, to "the treasure of the house of the LORD," and were placed "by the hand of Jehiel," that is, under his charge.

Ellicott identifies this Jehiel: he was the head of the Gershonite clan called Jehieli, set earlier in the book over "the treasures of the house of God," and it was under his charge that these gems were now laid up. He notes the same peculiar grammar, the article standing for a relative pronoun, running through the surrounding verses, so that the whole passage speaks with one voice of those "with whom" precious stones "were found." So the gift of precious stones was not left loose but committed to a named and appointed custodian, a Levite whose family held exactly this trust. And the whole scene, Ellicott observes, has a precedent: it recalls the similar contribution of the princes at the making of the tabernacle, when the rulers brought the onyx stones and the stones to be set, recorded in Exodus 35:27.

Brief as the notice is, the witnesses let it round out the picture of the offering. Not everyone gave gold or silver by the talent; some brought what they had, the precious stones found in their keeping, and these too were received and carefully stored for the beautifying of the house. The verse quietly widens the circle of givers beyond the great sums of the princes to include every kind of treasure, each laid up under the hand of a faithful Levite for the work to come. The building of the temple, like the building of the tabernacle before it, is furnished not from one purse but from the willing gifts of many, gathered and guarded against the day when Solomon shall put them to use.
9:
The offering done, the mood of the assembly rises to joy, and the witnesses dwell on the peculiar gladness of a people glad to give. The reformed John Gill catches the double delight: they rejoiced not only that they had something to offer, but that they had hearts to do it; they found themselves entirely free in the work, and saw the same freedom in others, and this gave them extreme pleasure. The joy, in other words, is not merely over the gift but over the willingness, the discovery that their own affections ran gladly toward the house of God.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the phrase "with perfect heart," the wholeness of devotion that David had earlier charged upon Solomon, and compares the exultation to the song of Deborah in Judges 5:1. The rejoicing is corporate and unforced, the natural overflow of a nation that has given with one mind.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the joy on three levels at once. The people rejoiced, he says, because their willingness was itself an effect of God's grace working in them, an eminent token of God's favour toward them, and a pledge that this long-desired work would receive a certain and speedy accomplishment. And David's own "great joy" Wesley grounds in the same hope: to see the work on which his heart was so set now likely to go forward. It is, he adds movingly, "a great reviving to good men when they are leaving the world, to see those they leave behind zealous for the work of God."

The witnesses do not divide; together they turn a simple notice of rejoicing into a meditation on holy gladness. The people's joy is traced to grace, read as a sign of favour, and taken as a pledge of the future; the king's joy is the peace of a dying man who sees his life's great desire safe in willing hands. Behind both stands the conviction Gill and Wesley share, that the power to give freely is itself God's gift, so that the very willingness which occasions the joy is one more mercy to be joyful over. The scene closes the account of the offering not with a total but with a feeling, the settled, grateful gladness of a people and a king at one in the service of God.
10:
The offerings gathered, the aged king lifts his heart in the great thanksgiving that crowns his reign, and the witnesses gather with reverence around its opening blessing. The reformed John Gill grounds the whole prayer in David's theology of grace: he blessed the LORD before all the congregation because he ascribed to God's goodness both the ability and the willingness of himself and his people to give after such a manner; he knew it was God who wrought in them both to will and to do, and therefore gave him the glory of it. The praise is not self-congratulation dressed as piety but the honest confession of a man who traces every good motion back to its source.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the scene with dignity: David gives fitting expression to his joy at the success of the deepest wish of his heart in a prayer with which he closes the last great assembly of his reign. Since by divine decree not he, the man of war, but his son the peace-king Solomon was to build the temple, David had done all he could to prepare the way; and finding the princes willing to further it, the grey-haired servant of the Lord saw in their willingness a special proof of divine favour, for which he must thank God before the whole congregation. He praises, they note, "the God of Israel our father," or, as the prayer will later say, the God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the grammar of that address. "Before all" means "to the eyes of all," openly, as in Genesis 23:11; and the phrase "LORD God of Israel our father" binds "our father" to Israel the patriarch, not to Jehovah, for the fatherhood of God, though occasionally affirmed by the prophets, hardly became a ruling idea within Old Testament times. "For ever and ever," he adds, is literally "from eternity even unto eternity," the very cadence of the doxologies that close the books of the Psalter, as at Psalms 41:13.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, whose long meditation spans this whole prayer, seizes on the fitness of praise from a dying saint. David, now old and near his end, blesses the Lord not alone in his closet but before all the congregation; and it well becomes aged and dying saints, Henry says, to have their hearts enlarged in praise and thanksgiving, for this will silence their complaints of bodily weakness and make the prospect of death less gloomy. He notes that David's psalms toward the end of the book are most of them psalms of praise: "the nearer we come to the world of everlasting praise, the more we should speak the language and do the work of that world." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley echoes him almost word for word, David was now full of days and near his end, and the nearer we come to the land of everlasting praise, the more we should speak its language. The witnesses do not divide; they stand together in wonder at an old king who, with the grave in view, spends his failing breath in blessing God.
11:
The heart of the doxology now sounds, "Thine, O LORD, is the greatness, and the power, and the glory, and the victory, and the majesty," and the witnesses vie to unfold the ascription in which David lays every crown at God's feet. The reformed John Gill reads it as a confession that God possesses all: all greatness and immensity, all dignity of nature and perfection, all almighty power and excellent glory, all superiority to every being; and whatever of might or honour or majesty men appear to have, their grandeur and their victories are alike from God. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears the same self-emptying: the point of these verses, he says, is that David arrogates nothing to himself, but with the humility of genuine greatness ascribes everything to God, as if to say, "the greatness of my kingdom, the prowess of my warriors, the splendour of my throne, are thine, for thine are all things."

Ellicott lingers over the several words. "Greatness" (gedullah) is a late term; "power" is properly manly strength, then valour or prowess; "glory" is ornament, beauty, splendour; and "victory" is a word meaning glory or splendour, though the Syriac, the Septuagint and the Vulgate all render it "victory" here. The whole ascription, he notes, breathes the air of the songs of heaven, and he sets beside it the great doxologies of Revelation 4:11. The kingdom David names is the universal sovereignty of God, who is "exalted as head above all," the very headship confessed in Deuteronomy 28:13.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse together with the next, hearing in "for all that is in the heaven and in the earth is thine" the ground of the whole: because everything belongs to God, riches and honour come of him, he reigns over all, and in his hand it lies to make all things great and strong. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enriches the passage with the Targum's expansive paraphrase, which turns the bare attributes into a recital of God's mighty deeds, that he created the world by his great power, led the fathers out of Egypt, brought them through the Red Sea, appeared in glory on Sinai amid troops of angels, gave the victory over Amalek and over Sihon and Og, and by the splendour of his majesty made the sun stand still upon Gibeon until Israel was avenged.

The witnesses do not divide. Each in his own key sounds the single note of the verse: that all greatness, power, glory, victory and majesty are God's own possession, held in and of himself, and that every glory a king or a nation seems to own is borrowed from the fountain of all glory. David, at the summit of his reign and the end of his life, stands before the assembled greatness of Israel only to declare that none of it is finally his, it is the LORD's, and the LORD's alone.
12:
The ascription flows on into its practical edge, "both riches and honour come of thee," and the two witnesses press the sovereignty of God down into the ordinary distribution of human fortune. The reformed John Gill will not let a single advantage of men be traced to themselves. Whatever riches or honour the children of men possess, he says, is not owing to their merit, nor to their diligence and industry, nor to their wise conduct, but to the providence of God; and he notes, with a glance at the nations, that even the heathen confessed their gods to be the givers of riches. The wealth that David and his princes have just poured out was, in the end, God's own gift returning to God's own house.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms and refines the reading. "Both riches and honour come of thee" is literally "the riches and the honour are from before thee," the same confession made of Solomon's God-given wealth in 1 Kings 3:13. The two words the English renders "power and might," he observes, are the very pair rendered "might" and "power" in the previous verses, so that the doxology circles back upon its own vocabulary. And the closing clause, "in thine hand it is to make great, and to give strength unto all," he sets beside the song of Hannah and the song of Mary, "the LORD maketh poor, and maketh rich; he bringeth low, and lifteth up," in 1 Samuel 2:7-8, and "he hath put down the mighty from their seats, and exalted them of low degree," in Luke 1:52.

Brief as the verse is, the two witnesses converge on a single truth that reaches beyond the moment. It is God who raises men to greatness and God who arms them with strength; the honours of the throne and the riches of the treasury alike descend from his hand. David, having confessed that all things belong to God, now confesses that all advancement comes from God, so that the very power which enabled the offering, and the very honour which the offering adorned, were themselves on loan from the Giver. The doxology thus prepares its own humbling conclusion: if riches and honour and strength are all of God, then who is David, and what is his people, that they should have anything to give?
13:
The prayer turns from adoration to thanksgiving, "Now therefore, our God, we thank thee, and praise thy glorious name," and the witnesses find even in this short doxology a note of enduring, ever-renewed praise. The reformed John Gill roots the thanks in wonder at condescension: that God, who is so great and so far above them, should take any notice of them at all, and yet bestow so many great and good things upon them. Thanksgiving, for Gill, is the natural answer of the small and unworthy to a God who stoops to bless them.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fasten on the Hebrew participles. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the word for thanking, modim, from the verb to confess or praise, characterizes this praising of God as an enduring praise, always rising anew, not a single act but a continual offering. Charles Ellicott presses the same grammar: "we are thanking thee, and praising," the present participles marking an ongoing action; and he notes as a philological curiosity that this form modim occurs nowhere else in Scripture, though the verb is common in other shapes, and that the phrase "thy glorious name," literally "the name of thy glory," is likewise found only here. He sets beside it the kindred prayer that God's glorious name be blessed for ever, in Psalms 72:19.

The witnesses do not divide, and the little verse gathers a large meaning. The giving is done, the sums are counted, and now the whole assembly's response resolves into thanks, not thanks that ends, but thanks that keeps rising, praise offered to a name too glorious ever to be adequately praised. Gill supplies the ground of it, the astonishment that so great a God regards so small a people; Keil and Ellicott supply its character, a praise perpetually beginning again, so peculiar in its wording that the grammatical-historical witnesses can find no exact parallel to it elsewhere in Scripture. The rareness of the phrase becomes, in their reading, a mark of its fervour: David reaches for language never used before to name a gratitude that has no natural end. Together they make of the verse a hinge: David has confessed that all is God's and all comes from God, and the only fitting sequel is a thanksgiving that will never be finished, because the goodness that occasions it will never be exhausted.
14:
At the height of the giving David sinks lowest in himself, "But who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able to offer so willingly after this sort?", and the witnesses gather around the humility that answers the doxology. The reformed John Gill answers the king's question in his own terms: who am I, originally dust and ashes, a sinful creature, unworthy to receive anything from God, and much more unworthy of the honour of doing anything for him? The greatness of the gift only deepens the sense of the giver's littleness.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the theology of the confession: man of himself can give nothing, so that the question "who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able to show ourselves so liberal?" answers itself in the closing clause, "from thy hand," that which is received from thee, "have we given." They note the peculiar idiom rendered "be able," literally "to hold strength together," and the verb "offer willingly," hitnaddeb, to show oneself willing, especially in giving. Charles Ellicott adds that this phrase "to keep strength" is confined to a handful of passages in Chronicles and Daniel, and that "all things come of thee" means "from thee is the whole" of our wealth and our power, the very ground of the wonder.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the double gift behind the offering. John Wesley observes that to be able "to offer" at all required two things of God: that he should give them riches to bring, and a willing heart to bring them, both of which are the gifts and fruits of his grace and mercy; and "of thine own have we given thee" means "we return only what we have received, and therefore only pay a debt to thee." From this he draws his paradox: "the more we do for God, the more we are indebted to him, for the honour of being employed in his service, and for grace enabling us in any measure to serve him." Adam Clarke sets the seal of the Targum on the same thought: "from thy presence all good comes, and of the blessings of thy hands have we given thee."

The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn the climax of the offering into the deepest self-abasement of the reign: the richest gift ever laid before the LORD is confessed to be nothing but his own returning to him, and the willing hearts that gave it are confessed to be his own workmanship. David, at the summit of his generosity, discovers that he has given God nothing that God did not first give him, and that even the giving was grace. So the doxology bends back upon itself, and the king who has just called God the fountain of all greatness now owns himself but dust, enriched and made willing by the very hand he seeks to honour.
15:
The confession of nothingness deepens into a meditation on mortality, "for we are strangers before thee, and sojourners, as were all our fathers: our days on the earth are as a shadow, and there is none abiding," and the witnesses gather around both the pilgrim image and a disputed final clause. The reformed John Gill reads "strangers and sojourners" against Israel's tenure of Canaan: though they possessed the land, they held it not in their own right, but as the LORD's. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same with force, the land we possess is thine, not ours; we are not the proprietors but only thy tenants, and as our fathers were once mere strangers in it, so we at this day are no better before thee, having no absolute right but only leave to sojourn for the short time we live.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch ground the image in the law of the land: God had given Israel only the usufruct, not the freehold; and as of the land, so of all a man's property, it is only a gift committed to us in trust. The truth that our life is a pilgrimage, they say, citing Hebrews 11:13, is pressed home by the brevity of life, "as a shadow, so swiftly passing away, are our days upon the earth," as in Job 8:9. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke unfolds the same figure: we are here as those who lodge for a night in another's house; our days are continually declining, fading, passing away.

Over the last clause the witnesses genuinely differ. The Authorized rendering, followed in substance by Gill and Wesley, gives "there is none abiding," no earthly form endures, all is in decay. But Keil and Delitzsch, Charles Ellicott and Adam Clarke read the Hebrew word (mikveh) not as "abiding" but as "hope" or "expectation": Keil renders "there is no trust" in the continuance of life, citing Jeremiah 14:8; Clarke, "no expectation that we shall be exempt from the changes our fathers underwent"; and Ellicott bluntly, "there is no hope," no assured future, no outlook of permanence. The one reading looks at the world's impermanence, the other at man's want of any hope of enduring; but both, the witnesses agree, drive home the same lesson.

That lesson the witnesses press home together, and Wesley states it plainly: David's days had as much substance in them as most men's, for he was a good man, a useful man, and now an old man, and yet he puts himself in the very front of those who must own that their days on earth are but a shadow, a vain, dark, transient life that will end either in perfect light or perfect darkness. The king pleads his own mortality as the reason he can boast of nothing: what merit can there be in the offering of a shadow that is even now passing away?
16:
David returns to the great heap of treasure and confesses its true owner, "all this store that we have prepared to build thee an house for thine holy name cometh of thine hand, and is all thine own." The reformed John Gill pauses over the sheer size of the store, retailing the older reckonings that the gold and silver together amounted, by one computation, to tens of thousands of tons, a mass so vast it can only be called, as the Hebrew does, a "multitude." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms the sense of the word: "all this store" is strictly a multitude, and so a multitude of goods, riches; and the confession "cometh of thine hand, and is all thine own" is, he says, simply a clearer expression of the second half of v.14, the same truth restated, that what is given came first from God.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Keil and Delitzsch, reads the verse in one breath: all the riches which they had prepared for the building of the temple come from the hand of God. They note a small textual point, the written and read forms of the pronoun "it," the one neuter, the other agreeing with the "store," a scribe's nicety that does not touch the sense.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lifts the verse from temple-gold to a rule for the whole spiritual life. "All thine own," he says: in like manner we ought to acknowledge God in all spiritual things, referring every good thought, every good desire, and every good work to his grace. What David confesses of the gold and silver, that it was God's before it was given back, Wesley makes the pattern for every gift of the inner man, so that not even a holy wish may be claimed as our own.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they let the verse gather the whole prayer to a point. The store is enormous, but its size only sharpens the confession; the more there is to give, the plainer it is that none of it originated with the givers. David lays the mountain of treasure before God not as a benefactor presenting a gift but as a steward returning a trust, naming the holy name for which it was gathered and owning, in the same breath, that hand and store and all were God's from the first.
17:
The prayer moves inward, to the God who reads the heart, "I know also, my God, that thou triest the heart, and hast pleasure in uprightness," and the witnesses dwell on the sincerity David dares to plead before him. The reformed John Gill glosses the searching simply: thou triest the heart, that is, thou searchest it and knowest it perfectly, whether what is done proceeds from it or not. The offering is worth exactly what the heart behind it is worth, and God alone can weigh that.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out the boldness of the claim: before God, who searches the heart and loves uprightness, David can declare that he has given willingly and in uprightness of heart, and that the people also, to his joy, have shown the same willingness. The plural "which are present here," they note, refers to God's people gathered for the offering. Charles Ellicott sets the sincerity in its Hebrew colour: the word "uprightness" means straightness, as of a road, hence integrity, freedom from crookedness; and he draws the connexion tight: "Thou that lookest upon the heart knowest that my offering has been made without grudging and without hypocrisy; my motive was not my own interest, but thy glory," and hence the joyful thanksgiving over the free generosity of the people. He anchors "thou triest the heart" in the psalmist's confession at Psalms 11:4, and reads "which are present here" as literally "who have found themselves here."

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse marks a turn in the prayer from confession to conscience. Having owned that all he gave was God's own, David now appeals to the one witness who cannot be deceived: he claims no merit in the gift, but he does claim honesty in the giving, that it came from an upright and ungrudging heart, offered for God's glory and not for show. And his joy is doubled, for the same God who reads his heart has let him see the like uprightness in his people; the king rejoices not merely that the treasure was given, but that it was given sincerely, by hearts as straight as his own. Before the searcher of hearts, sincerity is the only thing a man may plead, and it is exactly this that David lays down alongside his gold.
18:
From thanksgiving David turns to petition, and the first prayer is that the people's willing spirit might last, "keep this for ever in the imagination of the thoughts of the heart of thy people, and prepare their heart unto thee." The reformed John Gill marks the ground of the appeal in the name invoked: the LORD God of Abraham, Isaac and Israel, the covenant God of the fathers, who had ever been mindful of his promise to them and to their seed. David prays to the God who keeps covenant, and asks him to keep the people's hearts.

The grammatical-historical witnesses agree on the substance of the request. Keil and Delitzsch read it as a prayer that God would enable the people ever to retain this frame of heart, "this" being defined by what follows, the cast of the thoughts of their heart, and that he would "direct their heart unto himself," as Samuel bids Israel in 1 Samuel 7:3. Charles Ellicott renders the opening verb "preserve this for ever," and glosses "this" as the cast or frame of mind that has shown itself in the day's freewill offerings: David asks God to give permanence to the very disposition the offering revealed.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the theology of grace under the petition. Adam Clarke reads it as a plea grounded in gratitude: all the good dispositions David and his people have came from God in the first place, and so he prays, "continue to support and strengthen them by the same grace by which they have been inspired." John Wesley sharpens the last clause, arguing that "prepare their heart" is better rendered "confirm," "thou who hast begun a good work, confirm and carry it on by thy grace," and grounds the whole prayer in covenant: God is "in covenant with them, and with us for their sakes," and since it is from his grace that they have such willing minds, David asks that the grace may continue.

The witnesses do not divide; together they show a king who trusts nothing to the mere enthusiasm of a day. He has seen his people give with a perfect heart, and he knows how quickly such fervour cools; so his prayer is not that they might feel this once, but that God would fix the frame of their thoughts and confirm the willing mind he himself had given. The same grace that begins the good work, David pleads, must preserve it, a petition that quietly confesses how little the human heart can be trusted to keep even its own best resolutions without God to hold them fast.
19:
David's second petition is for his son and successor, "give unto Solomon my son a perfect heart, to keep thy commandments, thy testimonies, and thy statutes, and to do all these things, and to build the palace." The reformed John Gill glosses the "perfect heart" as one set to keep all the laws of God, moral, ceremonial and judicial, and to keep them cordially and sincerely, from the heart and not for show. The prayer is not for Solomon's wealth or fame but for his integrity.

The grammatical-historical witnesses read the same undivided heart. Keil and Delitzsch render it a "whole" or undivided heart, that Solomon might keep all the divine commands and do them, and build the temple; they note the phrase "to do all," that is, all that the commandments, testimonies and statutes require. Charles Ellicott agrees, glossing "to do all these things" as either "to do the whole" of God's commandments, or "to carry out all my designs," and reads "for the which I have made provision" as "which I have prepared to build." He anchors the triad of "commandments, testimonies and statutes" in the language of Deuteronomy 6:17.

The witnesses do not divide, and the petition rewards a closer look. David has just prayed for the people's steadfastness; now he prays for the king's integrity, and he joins two things in a single breath, the keeping of God's law and the building of God's house. Solomon is to build the temple, but only as one whose heart is whole toward God; the great architectural work and the obedient heart belong together, so that the house will be rightly built only by a builder rightly disposed. And there is a quiet poignancy in the prayer, for David had charged this very perfection upon Solomon earlier, and now turns the charge into a petition: what he had commanded his son to be, he now asks God to make him. The father knows that a perfect heart is not achieved by resolve but given by grace, and so he lays even his son's obedience, like the people's willingness, in the hand of God.
20:
The prayer ended, David calls the assembly to join him, and the witnesses attend to the double homage that follows, "and all the congregation... bowed down their heads, and worshipped the LORD, and the king." The reformed John Gill notes only the audience, the congregation of princes, captains and officers. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the tone of the summons: "Now bless" is really "Bless ye, I pray"; the "now" is not a note of time but of entreaty. The people answer by blessing the LORD God of their fathers, probably in a set liturgical formula like the doxologies that close the books of the Psalter, as at Psalms 41:13; and they "bowed and worshipped," that is, prostrated themselves, the two acts, Ellicott observes, being almost always joined.

On the striking last phrase, that they worshipped "the LORD, and the king," the witnesses take pains to keep the distinction clear. Keil and Delitzsch read the pair of verbs, bowing and worshipping, as a single connected gesture, made first before God and then before God's anointed. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states it plainly: they did reverence to God as the supreme Ruler, and to the king as his deputy. John Wesley puts it most crisply of all, "the Lord with religious, and the king with civil worship," so that the one act of homage carries two entirely different meanings, adoration toward heaven and honour toward the throne. Ellicott adds that David, as God's earthly representative, receives the same outward tokens of reverence, as Bathsheba had done obeisance in 1 Kings 1:31.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the moment into a lesson on true worship. Whoever is the mouth of the congregation, he says, those only have the benefit who join with him, not so much by bowing down the head as by lifting up the soul. Outward posture without inward devotion profits nothing; the bowed head is only worship when the soul is lifted with it. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show a whole nation, at the close of David's great prayer, uniting in a single act that honours God supremely and the king beneath him, the bent head the sign of an inward homage that, as Henry insists, must rise from the soul if it is to reach heaven at all.
21:
The day after the great assembly, the leaders seal their confession with sacrifice, and the witnesses count the offerings and weigh their kinds. The reformed John Gill notes simply that David and the congregation sacrificed. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the scene as a great sacrificial feast to the LORD: a thousand bullocks, a thousand rams, and a thousand lambs offered as burnt-offerings, with drink-offerings to correspond, and "sacrifices in abundance for all Israel," these last being thank-offerings, the shelamim, from which joyous sacrificial meals were prepared, so that all present could share. They note a nicety: the word "sacrifices" at the head of the verse is the general term for bloody offerings, but at the close, set beside the burnt-offerings, it narrows to mean the peace-offerings on which the people feasted.

Charles Ellicott confirms and dates the occasion: "on the morrow after that day" is a phrase found only here, meaning the day after the assembly; the count "bullocks a thousand, rams a thousand" follows the later Hebrew mode of speech; and the drink-offerings are libations poured with the sacrifices. The "sacrifices in abundance for all Israel," he agrees, are the thank-offerings of Deuteronomy 12:6, given so that every one present might partake of the sacrificial meal, the burnt-offering and the peace-offering both being ordained in Leviticus 1:1 and following. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds from the Targum that the drink-offerings too numbered a thousand, and agrees that the final sacrifices were peace-offerings, offered for the people, on the flesh of which they feasted.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they show the offering of treasure crowned by an offering of worship: having emptied their coffers for the house of God, the leaders now bring their thousands of victims, and the whole nation is drawn into the joy. The burnt-offerings ascend wholly to God; the peace-offerings are shared in a great feast; and so the day of giving passes into a day of communion, the people eating and rejoicing before the LORD on the very sacrifices they had brought. The gold was laid up for a temple not yet built; the sacrifice and the feast are enjoyed at once, a foretaste of the fellowship the finished house would house.
22:
The feast becomes a coronation, and the witnesses read the double anointing of king and priest, "they made Solomon the son of David king the second time, and anointed him unto the LORD to be the chief governor, and Zadok to be priest." The reformed John Gill sets the meal before the ark, in the tabernacle David had pitched for it, where they ate and drank before the LORD with great gladness. On the arresting phrase "the second time," the witnesses are agreed as to the first: it points back to the hurried anointing during Adonijah's attempt on the throne. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains that Solomon had first been anointed and proclaimed in haste, when his brother Adonijah affected the crown, by Zadok, Nathan and Benaiah, and without pomp; now that all was quiet, and David, Clarke thinks, probably now dead, they anointed and proclaimed him afresh, with due ceremony and sacrifice. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch both trace that first anointing to the brief notice in 1 Chronicles 23:1 and its full account in 1 Kings 1:32.

On the meaning of "anointed unto the LORD," the grammatical-historical witnesses agree it denotes not merely anointing in God's presence but anointing in accordance with his will, since it was God who had chosen Solomon; the king is the LORD's vicegerent, as Clarke says, God never having given up his own right to be King in Israel. But over the anointing of Zadok a real difference opens. Keil and Delitzsch take it that Zadok here received the high-priesthood outright, and with the consent of the estates of the kingdom; Ellicott calls the notice remarkable and peculiar to Chronicles, illustrating the almost sovereign dignity of the high priest's office and explaining the deposition of Abiathar as already contemplated by David, as 1 Kings 2:26 would show. John Wesley demurs: the anointing of Zadok, he argues, did not actually constitute him high priest, but only settled the reversion of the office upon him and his line after Abiathar's death, for Abiathar continued to exercise the priesthood until Solomon thrust him out, in 1 Kings 2:27.

The witnesses do not divide over the great fact, that on this glad day Solomon was confirmed king and Zadok marked out for the priesthood, the throne and the altar both settled before David died. But they weigh the priestly notice differently: was Zadok installed then and there, the estates consenting, or only designated, the actual succession waiting on Abiathar's fall? The one reads the verse as an accomplished consecration, the other as a secured reversion. Either way, the day that began in feasting ends in the orderly transfer of both crown and mitre, so that the kingdom David loved passes, king and priest together, into the hands God had appointed.
23:
The narrative now lifts its eyes from the deathbed of one king to the accession of the next, "then Solomon sat on the throne of the LORD as king instead of David his father, and prospered; and all Israel obeyed him." The reformed John Gill reads the phrase "the throne of the LORD" of the God who had given Solomon the throne, established him on it, and made him his vicegerent over his people. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat these verses as the necessary conclusion to the history of David's reign, showing how David's wishes for the son whom the LORD had chosen were fulfilled; "he prospered" answers exactly to the hope David had expressed, and "all Israel obeyed him," literally "gave the hand under him," means they submitted. Charles Ellicott adds that it is not meant that David abdicated; the notice of Solomon's reign here is anticipative, gathered in at this point to show how David's last wishes were realized.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on the meaning of that royal phrase. The throne of Israel is called "the throne of the LORD," he says, because God was in a peculiar manner the King of Israel, as Samuel had reminded the nation in 1 Samuel 12:12; he had the founding and the filling of their throne, their laws were divine, and Urim and prophets were the privy counsellors of their princes. And Henry lifts the type higher: Solomon's kingdom prefigured the kingdom of the Messiah, whose throne is indeed the throne of the LORD, for the Father judgeth no man but hath committed all judgment to the Son, and calls him his King in Psalms 2:6. Being set on the throne to which God called him, Solomon prospered, for those who follow the divine guidance may expect success by the divine blessing.

Henry marks too the marvel of a peaceable accession. All Israel obeyed Solomon, the princes, the mighty men, even the sons of David whose seniority might have given them a prior claim, and God inclined their hearts to submit, that his reign might from the first be peaceable; whereas David his father, a better man, had come to the crown only through much difficulty and many slow steps, having more faith and therefore more trial. The witnesses do not divide. Together they present Solomon seated, prospering, and universally obeyed, and they read his quiet enthronement as the visible answer to David's prayers and the fulfilment of God's choice, a throne called by God's own name, filled by God's own appointment, and secured by God's own inclining of every heart.
24:
The submission of the whole ruling class is recorded, and the witnesses fasten on the curious idiom for their homage, "all the princes, and the mighty men, and all the sons likewise of king David, submitted themselves unto Solomon the king," literally, "gave the hand under Solomon." The reformed John Gill identifies the parties, the princes of the tribes and the officers of the army. Charles Ellicott distinguishes the grandees from the royal house: "all the princes" are the great men of the realm, while the king's sons are named apart; and he reads in the whole notice an allusive glance at the recent attempt of Adonijah, put down in 1 Kings 1:5 and following.

Over the gesture itself the witnesses divide. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke takes "they gave the hand under Solomon" to mean they swore fealty to him, connecting it with the ancient form of oath in which the hand was put under the thigh, as Abraham's servant swore in Genesis 24:9. The grammatical-historical witnesses are more cautious. Keil and Delitzsch grant that "to give the hand under" means to submit, but expressly deny that the phrase derives from any oath-custom of clasping hands. Ellicott too holds it apart from the ordinary "giving the hand" in token of good faith, and takes it rather for some ancient mode of doing homage. So the question stands between them: is the "hand under Solomon" the gesture of a sworn oath of allegiance, or simply the sign of submission and homage, with no oath necessarily implied?

The witnesses do not press the difference into a quarrel, for on the substance they agree. Keil and Delitzsch note that the LORD magnified Solomon, the little word "all" in "no king before him" reaching back over Saul, Ishbosheth and David himself; and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, glossing the throne once more, calls it "the throne of the Lord" because the Lord himself was in a peculiar manner the King and Governor of Israel, who had the founding and the filling of it. Whether by oath or by homage, the whole leadership of the nation, princes, captains, and even the rival claimants among David's own sons, put their hands beneath the new king and owned his rule. The transfer David had prayed for is complete: not a dissenting voice is left, and the son sits secure on a throne that bears his God's name.
25:
The historian pauses to mark the singular glory God gave the new king, "and the LORD magnified Solomon exceedingly in the sight of all Israel, and bestowed upon him such royal majesty as had not been on any king before him in Israel." The reformed John Gill locates that magnifying chiefly in the gift for which Solomon became proverbial: God magnified him exceedingly by giving him so large a share of wisdom and understanding for government. The greatness is not merely outward splendour but the inward endowment of a ruler's mind.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording of the honour. "Bestowed upon him such royal majesty" is literally "put upon him a glory of kingship that had not become on any king over Israel before him"; the phrase "put glory upon" he traces to the psalm that speaks of God crowning man with glory and honour, in Psalms 8:2. And he notes soberly how few kings that "before him" can mean: only two, or counting Ish-bosheth three, had reigned in Israel at all, Saul, David, and the short-lived son of Saul, so that the comparison, grand as it sounds, is drawn against a very short line. The fuller measure of Solomon's God-given wisdom Ellicott finds promised in 1 Kings 3:12.

The two witnesses do not divide, and the brief verse carries the theme of the whole closing section. The exaltation of Solomon is God's doing, not Solomon's, "the LORD magnified" him, and God "bestowed" the royal majesty. Gill names the substance of it, wisdom to govern, the endowment that would make Solomon a wonder to the nations; Ellicott measures its unprecedented degree, a splendour no earlier king had worn, and points forward to the reign in which that promised wisdom and wealth would be seen in their fulness. Together they present the young king clothed in a greatness he did not win but received, the visible token that the God who chose him now honoured him before all Israel. And there is a fitness in the sequence, for the verse crowns the account of a father who ascribed all greatness to God with the picture of a son whom God now makes great: the doxology of v.11 is answered in the fact of v.25. The throne that bears the LORD's name is filled by a king the LORD himself has made great, so that Solomon's glory, like David's gold, is finally traced back to the hand that gave it.
26:
The chronicle turns at last to sum up the reign it has celebrated, "thus David the son of Jesse reigned over all Israel." The reformed John Gill remarks only that the reign, as the book and the earlier history had shown, was long and glorious. The grammatical-historical witnesses hear a deliberate emphasis in the words "over all Israel." Keil and Delitzsch note that the phrase looks back to the fact that David had at first been king over Judah alone, and only later was recognized by all the tribes; Charles Ellicott renders the verse "Now David had reigned over all Israel," and finds in it the same allusion to the antecedent reign over Judah only, recorded in 2 Samuel 5:1 and following.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley explains the placement of the summary. The sacred writer, having mentioned the anointing of Solomon and gone on to sketch his settlement in the kingdom, now returns to his main business, to give the account of the close of David's reign and life; he "brings him to the end of his day, leaves him asleep, and draws the curtains about him." It is an obituary, set down with a kind of tender finality.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry uses the summary to look back over the whole man. When we read the second book of Samuel, he says, we could scarcely have expected to behold David appear so illustrious in his closing scene; but his repentance had been as remarkable as his sin, and his conduct in his afflictions and toward the end of his life had a good effect on his subjects. From this Henry draws his gospel comfort: "even the chief of sinners may hope for a glorious departure, when brought to repent and flee for refuge to the Saviour's atoning blood." Let us mark the difference, he urges, between the man after God's own heart, living and dying, and worthless professors who resemble him in nothing but their sins; and let us watch and pray, lest we be overcome by temptation to the dishonour of God.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn a single sentence of summary into the gateway of David's obituary, a reign long and glorious, over a kingdom at last united, closed by a writer who lays the old king gently to rest, and read by Henry as the proof that grace can make even a great sinner's end illustrious.
27:
The reign is measured out in years, "the time that he reigned over Israel was forty years; seven years reigned he in Hebron, and thirty and three years reigned he in Jerusalem." The reformed John Gill passes over the verse with a bare cross-reference, letting the numbers speak. The grammatical-historical witnesses supply the detail. Keil and Delitzsch confirm the total of forty years, divided between the seven at Hebron and the thirty-three at Jerusalem, and carry the reckoning straight on to the death-notice that follows, where David dies in a good old age, full of days, riches and honour.

Charles Ellicott notes that this verse is virtually a duplicate of the register in 1 Kings 2:11, omitting only the name "David" at the beginning and the word "years" at the end; the Hebrew, he observes, opens simply with "the days that he reigned," the plain idiom for the span of a life. And he adds a small correction of the round figure: the seven years at Hebron were, more exactly, seven years and a half, as the fuller record in 2 Samuel 5:5 makes plain, the chronicler, as often, giving the whole number where the older history gives the fraction. The witnesses read no discrepancy into the rounding; the chronicler's forty and the earlier historian's forty and a half tell one story, and the compiler is content with the settled, memorable total.

The witnesses do not divide, and even this dry catalogue of years rewards attention. Forty years, the span of a generation, the length of Israel's wilderness wandering and of Moses' and of Solomon's reigns after him, measures the whole arc of David's kingship, from the fugitive crowned at Hebron over a single tribe to the aged monarch who ruled all Israel from Jerusalem. The two capitals mark the two halves of the story: the long, contested climb to the throne of Judah, and the settled dominion over the united nation from the city he made his own. The verse sets no verdict on the reign; it only numbers its days, and leaves the reader to feel, in the neat arithmetic of forty years, the full weight of a life now drawing to its close.
28:
The death itself is recorded with a fullness that dwells on the completeness of David's life, "and he died in a good old age, full of days, riches, and honour: and Solomon his son reigned in his stead." The reformed John Gill fixes the age at about seventy years, the good old age of a man who had run his course. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke unfolds the fourfold satisfaction: at his death David had everything his heart could wish, a good old age, having lived as long as living could be desirable and in the main enjoyed good health; full of days, having seen all he lived for either accomplished or in a fair way to it; full of riches, witness the immense sums left for the temple; and full of honour, having gained more renown than any crowned head before him or since, "laurels that are fresh to the present hour."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens one phrase: "full of days" is literally "satisfied with days," as the Septuagint and Vulgate render it, the same contentment ascribed to Abraham's death in Genesis 15:15; and the closing clause, "Solomon his son reigned in his stead," is the regular formula that runs from 1 Kings 11:43 onward through the whole history of the kings. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the spiritual sense from that word "satisfied": David had enough of this world and of its riches and honour, and, what is rarer, he knew when he had enough; he was satisfied with it, and very willing to go to a better place.

The witnesses do not divide, and their several notes gather into one portrait of a blessed end. The man who had been a soldier from his youth, his life continually in his hand, was not cut off in the midst of his days but preserved to a full old age and died in peace, laden with years and wealth and glory, and yet, as Wesley insists, satisfied rather than sated, holding all these things loosely because his heart was set on God. The dying king is "full," but not full of the world; he has enough of it, and is glad to leave it, and so passes from a throne that bore God's name to the God whose name it bore, while his chosen son sits down in his place.
29:
The historian points beyond his own pages to the older records of the reign, "now the acts of David the king, first and last, behold, they are written in the book of Samuel the seer, and in the book of Nathan the prophet, and in the book of Gad the seer." The reformed John Gill glosses "first and last" as all that David did that was memorable, both before he was king and when he was king, in Hebron and then in Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the Hebrew: "the acts" is dibre, the words or matters, the history of David; and the idiom is "written on," not "in," as the Hebrews said of writing upon a scroll or tablet, as at Exodus 34:1. Gesenius, he notes, rendered the whole "the deeds of king David... behold, they are written in the book entitled The Words of Samuel."

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses reflect on those three named authorities. Adam Clarke observes that David's acts, first and last, those of his private life as well as of his regal government, were written by three eminent men personally acquainted with him through the chief part of his life: Samuel and Gad the seers, and Nathan the prophet; and he adds the sober fact that these writings are all lost, save the particulars interspersed in the books of Samuel, Kings and Chronicles, none of which are the records here meant. John Wesley agrees that the reference is to the chronicles of the kingdom, written by Nathan and Gad, who were not only prophets but historiographers, out of which the sacred writers took, by the direction of God's Spirit, such passages as were most useful for the church in after ages.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw the sentence together, noting that the acts of David are said to be written "together with all his reign and his might, and the times that went over him," "the times," with their joys and their sorrows; and "the kingdoms of the lands" are the nations with which David came into contact: Philistia, Edom, Moab, Ammon and Aram. The witnesses do not divide. Together they open a window onto a lost library: a full record of David's reign once stood in the books of three prophets, of which only what Scripture preserved has come down to us, so that even this dry citation of vanished sources testifies to a life too large for any single book to hold.
30:
The book closes on the vicissitudes of a full and stormy reign, "with all his reign and his might, and the times that went over him, and over Israel, and over all the kingdoms of the countries." The reformed John Gill reads "his might" of the whole of David's mighty and valiant acts, the battles he fought and the victories he won. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that "might" here means valour or prowess, David's warlike achievements, the same word rendered "power" in the doxology at v.11; and "the times that went over him," he says, are the seasons of good and evil fortune, the vicissitudes of his own and his people's history. The "kingdoms of the countries" are the surrounding nations with which David had dealings of friendship or war. Ellicott notes too that the Syriac version adds a closing tribute, that David "did that which was good before the Lord, and departed not from anything that he commanded him all the days of his life."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke ends the book with a full estimate of the man, borrowing Delaney's summation: David was "a true believer, a zealous adorer of God, a consummate and unequalled hero, a skillful captain, a steady patriot, a wise ruler, a faithful and magnanimous friend, a true penitent, a divine musician, a sublime poet, and an inspired prophet, by birth a peasant, by merit a prince; in youth a hero, in manhood a monarch, and in age a saint." Yet Clarke will not gloss the great stain: "the matter of Uriah and Bathsheba is his great, but only, blot"; there he sinned deeply, and no man ever suffered more in body, soul and household in consequence. His penitence, Clarke insists, was as deep and extraordinary as his crime, and nothing could surpass both but the eternal mercy that took away the guilt and restored the humbled transgressor to holiness and happiness, so that he closes with a doxology of his own: "Let the God of David be exalted for ever!"

The witnesses do not divide, and their last word is fittingly double-edged. They set the reign's full sweep, its might, its victories, its dealings with the nations, and the changing times of fortune that passed over king and people alike, beside the honest record of the one deep fault and the deeper repentance that answered it. David departs not as a flawless idol but as a forgiven sinner, mighty in war and mightier in penitence; and the chronicle that began with genealogies from the first man ends, through Clarke's mouth, not in the praise of David but in the praise of David's God.
